ATTORNEYS AT LAv\,
(l892-r942)
( r9 r5- r979)
VIEW', CALIFOENIA O4O42
(415) 967_6941
April 9, 1992
Ms. Blanca Breeze
Staff Attorney
F air Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacram ento, CA 958 t4
Re: Request for eonflict of interest opinion
City of East Palo AIto
Dear Ms. Breeze:
This letter is a follow-up to our recent telephone diseussions coneerning
various conflict of interest questions arising from the employment of a city council
member by a non-profit organization owing real property within a designated
redevelopment area. Although you kindly provided telephonic advice at that time, we
would like to obt&in a written opinion on behalf of the FPPC, and for this purpose, I
witl attempt to outline the facts in more detail and set forth a list of particular
actions for whieh we would request a determination as to whether the councilmember
will be disqualified from partieipation.
The East Palo AIto Couneilmember in question, Ms. Nevida Butler, is
employed as a full time salaried staff employee of a non-profit corporation which has
qualified for tax exemption status under Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue
Code. The main aetivity of the corporation is distribution of food and clothing to
homeless and indigent persons in and around the City of East Palo Alto. The
organization is managed by a I7 member Board of Directors, all of whom are
volunteers. Ms. Butler is not a member of the Board, nor does she have any
ownership, proprietary, or other interest in the organization beyond her position as a
salaried employee. The non-profit corporation has gross annual receipts of
approximately $300,000.00, virtually all of which is derived from eontributions from
members of the public at large and institutional charities,
The members of the East PaIo Alto City Council also serve as the members
of the East Palo Alto Redevelopmant Agency. As of the time Ms. Butler was first
elected to the Council, the City and Redevelopment Agency had established the
University Circle Redevelopment Project Area, adopted General PIan amendments and
a Specific Plan for the Project Area, rezoned the Project Area to a classification
eonsistent with the General and Specific Plans, entered into a Disposition and
Development Agreement with a private developer for acquisition and development of
the Project Area as a mixed use office complex, and granted a planned Unit
Development (PUD) permit and tentative subdivision map approval to the developer.


Ms. Blanca Breeze
April 9, 1992
Page Two
The Council and Agency also certified a program EIR and a project EIR relating to the
foregoing legislative aetions and development approvals. The original project was
thereafter modified as a result of litigation commenced by neighboring cities objecting
to certain environmental impacts and these modifications were approved by the East
PaIo Alto Planning Commission. Consequently, at this point in time, all development
approvals for the modified project have been granted. The next stage of activity
would be the acquisition of the Project Area by the Redevelopment Agency (through
negotiation and eminent domain proceedings), relocation of the existing occupants,
transfer of the land to the developer, abandonment of existing public sireets within
the Project Area, and commencement of construction.
The non-profit corporation owns real. property within the University Circle
Redevelopment Project Area. This parcel represents I out of a total of 37 parcels of
land to be acquired by the Agency. The non-profit organization intends to continue its
aetivities at a new location within the immediate area. Ms. Butler will continue to be
employed throughout this process, The eorporation will be entitled to relocation
benefits provided by law, in addition to compensation for the fair market value of its
real property.
Although project approvals have already been granted, further actions by
the City Council and the Redevelopment Agency will be required for implementation
of such approvals and performance under the existing Disposition and Development
Agreement between the developer and the Agency. Moreover, it is possibla that
further changes will be made to the land use and design for a portion of the project.
Our questions with respect to these actions would include the following:
A. 
ACQI'ISITION OF BEAL PBOPEBTY BY THE AGENCY
In order to acquire each parcel of land within the project Area, the
Redevelopment Agency will authorize the initiation of negotiations with the
respeetive property owners (commeneing with the presentation to each individual
owner oJ an offer to purchase, based upon the appraised value of such ownerrs
property). If a-negotiated purchase is not accomplisnea, tne Agency will need to adopt
a Resolution of Necessity for the conduct of eminent Oomain pioceldings.
Based upon our telephone conversation, it would appear that Ms. Butler
should not participate in councit or Agency actions direcuy relaied to the acquisition
of the. specifie req]: property -owned by the non-profif corporation, wJ woura
appreciate your confirmation of this rulirig. Howevlr, since tfie u"quiritionit""""r,
parcel within the project Area is a separate transaction (and a'sLp"rate- r"grr
proceeding, if required), we believe that Ms. Bu er should not be disqualified from
participating in actions relating to such_ other acquisitionr. in"-eg*Jvli".-" ils",
obligation to pay the fair market value for each parcer, as determinE; oy inJepenaent
appraisal. Because the variors parcets within the project Area have oirr"r"ii *"r,
sizes, physical improvements and adjustmenir io-r"ir", sueh as contamination from
toxic materiars, the amount paid for one p"r""i oo"r not'necessar y t"r" "" ""o""*i"
e,ffect upon the amount paid-.for another F;;1.- T;r;; even rhowh it may be the case
that Ms. Buuer is disquarified from participaiin!' in aecisiin, -."l"liig'ii 
tt,"


Ms. Blanca Breeze
April 9, 1992
Page Three
acquisition of her employey's property, we woulld request your concurrenee with the
opinion that she is not disqualified from participating in decisions regarding acquisition
oi other properties within the Project Area in whieh her employer has no ownership
interest.
B. 
BELOCATIONOPBXISTINGOCCUPANTS
The acquisition and development of the University Circle Project Area will
require relocation of approximately 58 commercial establishments and the oceupants
of -approximately ?3 residentiat units. The non-profit corporation which employs
Councilmember Butler has been included in the group of "commereial establishmentsrl
for purposes of determining relocation benefits.
The Redevelopment Agency is required by law to adopt a relocation plan
establishing benefits and procedures for occupants of the Project Area who will be
displaced by the development. The nature and amount of financial and other
relocation benefits will be governed by statutory provisions set forth in the
Government Code and guidelines adopted thereunder by the State Department of
Housing and Community Development. In this ease, since a portion of the Project
Area *i[ be transferred to Caltrans for freeway purposes' the federal relocation
guidelines will also be applieable.
As in the case of property acquisition, we would assume that Ms. Butler
cannot participate in any decisiors relating to the specifie relocation benefits that
may be payable to her employer. We would appreciate your confirmation of this
opinion. AIso, we would raise the further question as to whether Ms. Butler can
participate in the decision to adopt or amend the general reloeation plan. It should be
noted that such plan contains dollar amounts and descriptions of moving costs and
expenses that are payable to businesses and non-profit organizations that are forced to
relocate. However, these amounts and descriptions are derived from the state and
federal law and guidelines. The benefits are intended to pay or reimburse the
occupant for the cost of the relocation so as to avoid any net loss (i.e. increase in
expenses) resulting from the displaeement. Moreover, the general rules apply equally
to all of the occupants of the Project Area. Although the Agency has authority to
increase such benefits and to resolve disputes and make interpretations concerning
entitlements, these matters would be handled on a case by case basis, thus giving Ms.
Butler the opportunity to abstain if a decision was needed with respect to benefits
claimed by her employer. Similarly, we feel Ms. Butler should be able to participate in
any case by case deeisions coneerning benefits claimed by residential or commercial
occupants other than her own employer because such decisions have no economic
effect upon her employer.
C. MODIFICATION OP PB(UECT LAND USES AND DESIGN
'The -a^pproved development project ineludes two pareels to be improved
with high-rise office structures Cnd a thlrd'parcel to be improved wit} a trotei. itrere
is a question as to whether a hotel is curren y feasible and it is likely that some form
of alternative proposal will be eonsidered for this parcel. It is also pissible that some


Ms. Blanca Breeze
April 9, 1992
Page Four
design and land use changes cotild be made to either of the office parcels. For
example, instead of a hotel, there might be a mixed use development consisting of
retail establishments and residential units. This type of ehange would require
amendment to the specific plan for the Project Area and corresponding amendments to
the PUD permit and development agreements. Since these changes have no economic
effect upon the acquisition of the property owned by Ms. Butlerrs employer, or the
relocation benefits payable to this non-profit corporation, we believe Ms. Butler should
not be disqualified from participating in any decisions concerning such project
modifications. We would request your concurrence with this opinion.
D. MISCELLANEOUSDECSIONS
Implementation of the approved project may involve various miscellaneous
decisions, including the following:
l. 
Granting or modification of agreements with project consultants,
For example, the Agency has existing contracts with an acquisition
consul.tant, a reloeation consultant, speeial legal counsel, and a
planning consultsnt.
Cooperative agreements with Caltans for the reconstruction of a
freeway interchB.nge, which is included as part of the development
project.
Professional service agreement with the County of San Mateo for the
performance of services on behalf of Caltrans for aequisition of
right-of-way.
4. 
Cooperative agreements with other public agencies and utilities
pertaining to the construction of public improvements required to
service the development or mitigate impacts from the project.
we do not believe that any of the implementing actions and agreements, such as those
listed above, would have an economic effeci upon the non--profit corporation that
employs Ms. Butler. We would request your concurrence with iis opinion,
To summarize the foregoing, we have raised the following questions:
(I) Is Ms. Buuer disquarified.from participating in decisions rerating to
the acquisition by the Redevelopment Agency of property o*n"d'by
her employer?
(2) Whether or not Ms. Bufler is disqualified under euestion I above, can
she p€rticipate in deeisions reiating to the icquisition of dttrer
parcels within the projeet Area not oined by trer erirptoyert
,


Ms. Blanca Breeze
April 9, 1992
Page F ive
(3) Can Ms. Butler participate in decisions to adopt or amend the general
relocation plan pertaining to the entire Project Area?
(4) Whether or not Ms, Butler is disqualified under Question 3 above, can
she participate in deeisions regarding case by case determinations on
relocation benefits claimed by occupants of the Project Area (other
than her own employer), based upon the provisions of the adopted
relocation plan?
(5) Can Ms. Butler participate in decisions regarding modifications to the
approved development project, such as changes in land use and
str ucture design?
(6) Can Ms. Butler participate in deeisions concerning implementation
and administration of the approved development project, such as
approval of ag?eements with consultants and other agencies.
If there is any additional information you require in connection with this
request, please do not hesitate to contact me by telephone. Your prompt response
would be greatly appreciated.
East Palo Alto City Attorney
HAROLD S. TOPPEL

sections.question:
A. ACQUISITION OF REAL PROPERTY BY THE AGENCY

In order to acquire each parcel of land within the project Area, the Redevelopment Agency will authorize the initiation of negotiations with the respective property owners (commencing with the presentation to each individual owner of an offer to purchase, based upon the appraised value of such owner's property). If a negotiated purchase is not accomplished, the Agency will need to adopt a Resolution of Necessity for the conduct of eminent domain proceedings.

B. RELOCATION OF EXISTING OCCUPANTS

The acquisition and development of the University Circle Project Area will require relocation of approximately 58 commercial establishments and the occupants of approximately 73 residential units.

C. MODIFICATION OF PROJECT LAND USES AND DESIGN

The approved development project includes two parcels to be improved with high-rise office structures and a third parcel to be improved with a hotel.

D. MISCELLANEOUS DECISIONS

Implementation of the approved project may involve various miscellaneous decisions, including consulting agreements, cooperative agreements with Caltrans, and professional service agreements.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether City Council member Nevida Butler, who is employed by a non-profit organization owning property within a redevelopment project area, is disqualified from participating in various City Council and Redevelopment Agency decisions regarding property acquisition, relocation benefits, project modifications, and implementing agreements.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from an attorney to FPPC, not an FPPC response letter. The document contains detailed facts and a numbered list of specific questions but no CONCLUSION or FPPC advice. The final five summarized questions appear to be truncated at the end of the provided text.

If a negotiated purchase is not accomplished, the Agency will need to adopt a Resolution of Necessity for the conduct of eminent domain proceedings.

B. RELOCATION OF EXISTING OCCUPANTS

C. MODIFICATION OF PROJECT LAND USES AND DESIGN

D. MISCELLANEOUS DECISIONS

Implementation of the approved project may involve various miscellaneous decisions, including consulting agreements, cooperative agreements with Caltrans, and professional service agreements.

embedding.summary: An attorney representing the City of East Palo Alto requests a written FPPC conflict of interest opinion regarding whether Council member Nevida Butler must recuse herself from decisions involving a University Circle Redevelopment Project, given her employment by a non-profit organization that owns property within the project area.
